payload.learning_objective: Understand that a tesseract is created by dragging a cube into the fourth dimension, and we can only see its 3D shadow/projection. The aha moment: the 4D construction process follows the exact same geometric logic as building a 3D cube from a 2D square — the pattern is identical, making 4D feel mechanically comprehensible.

mini_spec.title: Construction Recipe Comparison: 2D→3D vs 3D→4D
mini_spec.narrative_wrapper: On the left, you watch a square being dragged perpendicular to itself to sweep out a cube, with a progress slider showing each intermediate stage. On the right, the exact same slider simultaneously shows a cube being dragged through the fourth dimension to sweep out a tesseract. Every construction step happens in parallel, so you see the pattern: each dimension adds the same way.
mini_spec.manipulated_variable: Sweep distance controls how far the lower-dimensional shape has traveled perpendicular to itself, changing the completeness of the higher-dimensional object being constructed
mini_spec.primary_visual_feedback: Both panels animate synchronously: the left shows the square creating stacked copies connected by edges to form the cube; the right shows the cube creating stacked copies connected by faces to form the tesseract's structure
mini_spec.causal_chain_exposed: Numeric readouts show identical sweep distances; intermediate copies appear as ghosted layers; connecting elements (edges on left, faces on right) materialize progressively; labels count vertices, edges, faces, and cells in parallel
mini_spec.trace_strategy: The previous position of the swept shape remains visible as a faint ghost at the starting location, with all connecting elements persisting, creating a visual history ofthe sweeping motion and showing the complete path traveled
mini_spec.aha_moment: The student realizes that the 4D construction process is not mysterious—it follows the exact same geometric logic as building a 3D cube from a 2D square, just applied one dimension higher; the pattern is identical, making 4D feel mechanically comprehensible

payload.target_misconception: Students may think 4D is impossible or purely abstract with no visual representation
payload.section_context: Final section and the payoff. Students have built up: point → line → square → cube. Now they apply the same pattern to create a tesseract and see that it's not mysterious — it's the same construction method one dimension higher.
payload.design_brief: This is the climax of the tesseract lesson. The shared domain model in src/data/model.ts contains functions for tesseract vertices, edges, and 4D→3D projection. The tone is curious and triumphant — students should feel like they've unlocked understanding of 4D by recognizing the pattern. Use violet #AC8BF9 for this section. Show the side-by-side comparison so students see the pattern: square→cube on left, cube→tesseract on right, both controlled by the same slider. Count vertices (4→8 on left, 8→16 on right) to reinforce the doubling pattern.
